perturbation.original_text: Aimmune shall conduct the Development Activities in accordance with sound and ethical business and scientific practices, and in compliance with all Applicable Law, including GCPs and GLPs, and also including all applicable data privacy and data protection laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Aimmune shall conduct the Development Activities in accordance with sound business practices.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'ethical and scientific practices,' 'compliance with all Applicable Law, including GCPs and GLPs,' and 'all applicable data privacy and data protection laws,' this modification introduces a legal contradiction, as clinical trials and drug development are heavily regulated.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR Part 50 - Protection of Human Subjects
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation ensures the protection of human subjects in clinical trials. Omitting the requirement to comply with applicable laws directly contradicts the need for informed consent and ethical treatment of participants, creating a legal risk.

perturbation.justification: The modified text omits critical aspects of the original contract, specifically the requirement to conduct development activities in compliance with 'all Applicable Law, including GCPs and GLPs, and also including all applicable data privacy and data protection laws.' This omission directly contradicts the need for ethical treatment of participants and adherence to legal and ethical standards in clinical trials. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 'General requirements for informed consent' within the context of clinical trials, implying that such consent is a legal requirement. By removing the explicit obligation to comply with applicable laws, the modified contract creates a situation where these legal requirements are not guaranteed, presenting a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Aimmune hereby agrees to save, indemnify, defend and hold Xencor, its Affiliates, and their respective directors, agents and employees harmless from and against any and all Losses arising in connection with any and all Claims resulting or otherwise arising from (i) any breach by Aimmune of any of its representations, warranties, covenants or obligations pursuant to this Agreement, (ii) [***], (iii) the negligence or willful misconduct by Aimmune (or its Affiliates, Sublicensees, subcontractors, wholesalers or distributors) or their respective officers, directors, employees, agents or consultants in performing any obligations under this Agreement, or (iv) the Development, Manufacturing, Packaging and Labeling or Commercialization of the Antibody or a Product hereunder during or after the Term (including, for clarity, any product liability Losses resulting therefrom) by Aimmune (or its Affiliates, Sublicensees, subcontractors, wholesalers or distributors) or their respective officers, directors, employees, agents or consultants, in each case except to the extent that such Losses are subject to indemnification by Xencor pursuant to Section 11.1.
perturbation.changed_text: Aimmune hereby agrees to indemnify Xencor from Losses arising from (i) a breach by Aimmune of its obligations pursuant to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By significantly limiting the scope of indemnification, this change omits critical protection for Xencor, particularly related to product liability. This creates a legal contradiction, since companies have a legal duty to ensure their products are safe, and this shift weakens Xencor's ability to be held harmless from Aimmune's potential failures.
perturbation.law_explanation: This California law establishes the general principle of negligence liability. By narrowly limiting Aimmune's indemnification obligations, the modified text potentially contradicts California law by weakening Xencor's protection against damages resulting from Aimmune's negligence related to product development or commercialization, particularly regarding product liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1714 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 3. OBLIGATIONS IMPOSED BY LAW [1708 - 1725] ( Part 3 enacted 1872. ) 1714. (a) Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill in the management of his or her property or person, except so far as the latter has, willfully or by want of ordinary care, brought the injury upon himself or herself. The design, distribution, or marketing of firearms and ammunition is not exempt from the duty to use ordinary care and skill that is required by this section. The extent of liability in these cases is defined by the Title on Compensatory Relief. (b) It is the intent of the Legislature to abrogate the holdings in cases such as Vesely v. Sager (1971) 5 Cal.3d 153, Bernhard v. Harrah’s Club (1976) 16 Cal.3d 313, and Coulter v. Superior Court (1978) 21 Cal.3d 144 and to reinstate the prior judicial interpretation of this section as it relates to proximate cause for injuries incurred as a result of furnishing alcoholic beverages to an intoxicated person, namely that the furnishing of alcoholic beverages is not the proximate cause of inj
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly reduces the scope of Aimmune's indemnification obligations to only cover breaches of the agreement, omitting other liabilities like negligence or product liability. This contradicts California Civil Code Section 1714(a), which states that "Everyone is responsible, not only for the result of his or her willful acts, but also for an injury occasioned to another by his or her want of ordinary care or skill." By limiting indemnification, the changed text weakens Xencor's protection against damages resulting from Aimmune's negligence, particularly regarding product development or commercialization, which conflicts with the responsibility imposed by law.

perturbation.original_text: Aimmune shall procure and maintain insurance, including clinical trials insurance and product liability insurance, adequate to cover its obligations hereunder and which is consistent with normal business practices of prudent companies similarly situated at all times during which the Product is being clinically tested in human subjects or commercially distributed or sold by Aimmune pursuant to this Agreement; provided, that any such clinical trials insurance coverage shall, prior to the First Commercial Sale of a Product, in no event be less than [***] Dollars ($[***]) per loss occurrence, and product liability insurance coverage shall, after such First Commercial Sale, in no event be less than [***] Dollars ($[***]) per loss occurrence.
perturbation.changed_text: Aimmune shall procure and maintain insurance to cover its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for specific insurance types and coverage amounts creates a legal risk. Clinical trials and product liability insurance are necessary to protect parties from significant financial risks. This omission creates a legal contradiction with regulations that mandate insurance coverage for clinical trials.
perturbation.law_explanation: This regulation specifies the responsibilities of sponsors, including ensuring proper monitoring and financial protection during clinical trials. Omitting the clinical trial insurance requirement weakens the protection for subjects and contradicts federal regulations regarding clinical trial oversight and patient safety.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 CFR § 312.52 - Transfer of obligations to a contract research organization. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 21 CFR § 312.52 - Transfer of obligations to a contract research organization. CFR prev | next § 312.52 Transfer of obligations to a contract research organization . (a) A sponsor may transfer responsibility for any or all of the obligations set forth in this part to a contract research organization . Any such transfer shall be described in writing. If not all obligations are transferred, the writing is required to describe each of the obligations being assumed by the contract research organization . If all obligations are transferred, a general statement that all obligations have been transferred is acceptable. Any obligation not covered by the written description shall be deemed not to have been transferred. (b) A contract research organization that assumes any obligation of a sponsor shall comply with the specific regulations in this chapter applicable to this obligation and shall be subject to the same regulatory action as a sponsor for failure to comply with any obligation assumed under these regulations. Thus, all references to “sponsor” in this part apply to a contract research organization to the extent that it assumes one or more obligations of the sponsor . CFR Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The original text mandates specific insurance types (clinical trials and product liability) and coverage amounts, whereas the changed text only requires 'insurance to cover its obligations hereunder.' This omission contradicts the law explanation, which states that removing clinical trial insurance weakens protection for subjects and conflicts with regulations regarding clinical trial oversight and patient safety. The scraped law snippets address the transfer of obligations to contract research organizations, indicating that specific regulations apply to those assuming sponsor obligations. While the snippets do not explicitly detail required insurance types/amounts, the change creates a legal loophole by removing the clear mandate for these protections, increasing risk and violating implied protections. Therefore, the omission of mandated insurance types constitutes a legal contradiction, as it weakens necessary safeguards.
